FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, 21 recruitment sites, baseline ages 9-10, data release 5.1 explicitly stated in methods.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using longitudinal random-intercept cross-lagged panel model (RI-CLPM) on ABCD data across 4 waves.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in JAMA Network Open (2025); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: 'time spent on social media' (SM-specific variable) paired with depressive symptoms (CBCL mental health outcome) in RI-CLPM. Social media as IV: β = 0.07 (year 1→year 2, p = .01) and β = 0.09 (year 2→year 3, p < .001). Bidirectional model also estimates depressive symptoms as IV predicting subsequent social media use.

FT_IC3.evidence: Cross-lagged path coefficients (β) with 95% CIs and p-values reported for SM↔depressive symptoms relationship in RI-CLPM.

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results reported: β = 0.07 (95% CI 0.01-0.12), β = 0.09 (95% CI 0.04-0.14), and null cross-lagged paths from depression to SM use.

FT_EC7_reason: Bidirectional RI-CLPM design: social media appears as both IV (predicting depressive symptoms) and DV (predicted by depressive symptoms). EC7 requires SM to be DV-only with no SM-as-IV analysis; that condition is not met here.
notes: Clean INCLUDE. RI-CLPM with within-person SM use as predictor of depressive symptoms and vice versa. Main finding: within-person increases in SM use prospectively associated with greater depressive symptoms 1 year later.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, nationwide longitudinal cohort, ABCD 5.0 release, baseline 2016-2018 explicitly stated.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective analysis of ABCD data using multivariable linear regression and mediation models.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Social Psychiatry and Psychiatric Epidemiology (2025); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: social media screen time subtype reported as IV with own coefficient (B = 0.20, 95% CI 0.09-0.32, p = 0.001) predicting manic symptoms (7 Up Mania scale, mental health outcome). Texting also separately reported (B = 0.18, 95% CI 0.08-0.28, p < 0.001). Both are phone/SM-specific variables paired with an individual-trait mental health outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression B coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values reported for SM and texting subtypes predicting manic symptoms; indirect effect estimates from mediation analyses also quantified.

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative numeric results reported throughout (e.g., social media B = 0.20, texting B = 0.18, indirect effect of SM through SMAQ B = 0.12, 95% CI 0.09-0.16).

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time (including social media subtype) is the IV in all prospective main analyses predicting manic symptoms. EC7 requires SM to be DV-only; here SM is consistently IV.
notes: Clean INCLUDE. Prospective associations of screen time subtypes (social media, texting, videos, video games) with manic symptoms over 2 years; SMAQ used as mediator (not as the SM-side IV/DV pair that qualified IC2).

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, year 3 follow-up 2019-2021, 21 sites, data release 5.0 explicitly stated.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional logistic regression analysis of ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics (2025); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: The analysis pairs transgender/gender-questioning identity (IV) with cyberbullying victimization (DV). Cyberbullying alone does not qualify as a phone/SM variable per the binding clarification (clarification 3: 'cyberbullying alone fails IC2'). Time spent on the internet is included only as a covariate in supplemental sensitivity analyses, not as an IV or DV with its own main analysis coefficient. No qualifying phone/SM-specific variable (time on SM, SM platform, texting, phone use, SMAQ, MPIQ, etc.) appears as IV or DV in any main or sensitivity analysis.

notes: Excluded under FT-EC2. Paper studies cyberbullying victimization as a function of transgender/gender-questioning identity. Cyberbullying alone does not constitute a qualifying SM/phone variable. Internet time appears only as a covariate (not IV or DV) in supplemental models.
